Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed verbal phrase: perfective prefix nū- + infinitive verb + plural accusative noun; also inflects by tense/person when used in a sentence.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To give up, lose motivation, or stop trying after difficulty.
Usage Contexts: daily speech; school; family advice; workplace encouragement; sports
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: A rural-work metaphor: lowered hands signal that a person has stopped working or helping; common in Latgalian everyday moral speech.
First Example Sentence: Nūlaist rūkys nav laika, jo sēkla jau ībārta i drīz juosōk ravēt.
Second Example Sentence: Skolūtuoja sacieja, lai mēs nanūlaižam rūkys pēc pyrmuos kļūdys.
Third Example Sentence: Jis beja pīguojis, bet rūkys nanūlaida i pabeidza remontu leidz vakaram.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed verbal phrase: infinitive verb + singular accusative noun + prepositional phrase; the verb inflects, while the object phrase stays stable.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To remain silent, keep a secret, or avoid saying something harmful.
Usage Contexts: family; gossip; workplace discretion; humor; conflict avoidance
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Built on the bodily image of the tongue physically held back by the teeth; widely transparent in Latgalian oral interaction.
First Example Sentence: Ka sābru pogolmā sōka klačuot, vacōmāte maņ pamōja turēt mēli aiz zūbim.
Second Example Sentence: Sapulcē jis turieja mēli aiz zūbim, leidz direktors pats pajautōja vīduokli.
Third Example Sentence: Par puorsteigumu dzimšonys dīnā turit mēli aiz zūbim, lai Agnese nazkū nūjauš.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed verbal phrase: infinitive verb + singular accusative noun + plural locative noun; transitive metaphorical construction.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To mislead someone, obscure the truth, or speak in a way that hides the real situation.
Usage Contexts: politics; advertising; negotiations; family disputes; sarcasm
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: The image comes from fog blocking sight; it fits Latgale’s landscape imagery and is productive in public and household speech.
First Example Sentence: Kandidats pūta miglu acīs, solīdams ceļus bez naudas i bez projekta.
Second Example Sentence: Napūt maņ miglu acīs, es poša redzieju, kas nūtyka pi šķyuna.
Third Example Sentence: Reklama tik skaisti pūta miglu acīs, ka pat vaca mašyna izavēre kai jauna.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Comparative idiomatic clause: kai + prepositional phrase + infinitive; often used as a predicate complement.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To talk to someone who refuses to listen or respond meaningfully.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: runōt ar cylvāku, kas naklausōs voi nareaģej
Usage Contexts: family arguments; teaching; bureaucracy; workplace frustration; humor
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Stone walls and old masonry are familiar rural and town images; the idiom turns unresponsiveness into a physical barrier.
First Example Sentence: Es jam skaidroju treis reizis, bet tyka kai ar mūru runōt.
Second Example Sentence: Ar tuo īstuodi kai ar mūru runōt: viestulis aizīt, atbiļdis nav.
Third Example Sentence: Māte pasmējēs, ka ar pusaudzi rītā ir kai ar mūru runōt.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Idiomatic finite clause: nominative noun subject + prepositional phrase + finite perfective verb; tense and person can vary by context.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A person feels great relief after worry, fear, or uncertainty ends.
Usage Contexts: health news; exams; travel; family worries; emotional storytelling
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: The heart as a seat of feeling is prominent in Latgalian folklore and religiously colored speech; the stone image intensifies emotional weight.
First Example Sentence: Kad daktere pateica, ka analīzis lobys, akmins nu sirds nūkrita vysai saimei.
Second Example Sentence: Maņ akmins nu sirds nūkrita, ka dāls pazvaneja nu ceļa i sacieja, ka vysur ir drūši.
Third Example Sentence: Pēc eksamena rezultatu sajemšonys studentim akmins nu sirds nūkrita.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Idiomatic finite clause: singular noun subject + perfective finite verb + plural locative noun; vivid body-location metaphor.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To become suddenly frightened, shocked, or deeply alarmed.
Usage Contexts: accidents; ghost stories; unexpected news; humor; oral narratives
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: A bodily fear metaphor used in storytelling: emotion is imagined as dropping from the chest to the feet.
First Example Sentence: Kad naktī pi lodža kasīji zari, maņ sirds nūkrita papēžūs.
Second Example Sentence: Viņai sirds nūkrita papēžūs, ieraugūt, ka pase palikuse sātys.
Third Example Sentence: Bērnim sirds nūkrita papēžūs, kad pērkūns iesita tyvu kūtei.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed verbal phrase: infinitive verb + singular accusative noun + singular locative noun; object can be possessively modified in use.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To put oneself at risk for another person or cause.
Usage Contexts: politics; friendship; family loyalty; workplace responsibility; heroic narrative
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Rooted in hearth and fire imagery familiar in village households; the hand represents personal agency and danger.
First Example Sentence: Prīkšnieks bōza dūgu ugunī par sovim ļaudim, lai tikai nūsturātu dorbnīceibu.
Second Example Sentence: Tu navar vysu laiku bōzt dūgu ugunī par cylvāku, kas pats nanāk paleigā.
Third Example Sentence: Viņa bōza dūgu ugunī, aizstōvādama kaimiņini pret nataisneibu.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed verbal phrase: infinitive verb + singular accusative noun + singular locative noun; metaphorical object-complement pattern.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To secretly hold a grudge or keep resentment hidden while acting outwardly calm.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: slāpt aizvainuojumu; paturēt dusmys pret kūdu
Usage Contexts: family history; village relations; workplace tension; literature; irony
Register and Tone: colloquial, slightly archaic, ironic or suspicious
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: The hidden stone evokes a concealed weapon or burden; it suits Latgalian narratives about long memory, honor, and neighborly tension.
First Example Sentence: Jis smaida pi golda, bet es jytu, ka akmini azūte tur vāl nu pērnuos strīda.
Second Example Sentence: Naturi akmini azūte, labōk izrunōjim vysu tagad.
Third Example Sentence: Ciema ļaudis zynōja, ka starp bruoļim akmins azūte turāts jau godim.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed verbal phrase: infinitive verb + singular accusative noun + singular locative noun phrase; transparent search metaphor.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To look for something extremely difficult to find because the search area is too large or disordered.
Usage Contexts: daily speech; archives; technology troubleshooting; household search; humor
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Haymaking is central in rural Latgalian life; the tiny needle against a haystack gives a concrete agrarian scale to difficulty.
First Example Sentence: Bez dokumenta numura arhīvā meklēt tuo lītu ir kai adotu siena kaudzē.
Second Example Sentence: Es pogolmā pazaudieju mazu skrūveiti, i tagad meklēju adotu siena kaudzē.
Third Example Sentence: Internetā atrast patīsu atsauci reizēm ir adotu siena kaudzē meklēt.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Proverbial nominal clause: noun subject + negated copula + noun predicate; often followed by explanatory consequence.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Once words are spoken, they cannot easily be taken back, so speech should be careful.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: runō apduomōti, jo pateiktū gryuti atsaukt
Usage Contexts: moral advice; family; public speech; literature; conflict resolution; teaching
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: A bird-flight proverb type known across Eastern European oral traditions; in Latgalian it fits parish, family, and storytelling contexts where spoken reputation matters.
First Example Sentence: Vacōtāvs pīminieja, ka vōrds nav zvirbulis, todēļ dusmōs lobōk paklusēt.
Second Example Sentence: Pirms raksti tīklā, atguodoj: vōrds nav zvirbulis, izlaidīsi i vairs nanuķersi.
Third Example Sentence: Sātā bērnim mōceja, ka vōrds nav zvirbulis i cylvāku var ievainuot dziļuok par pātagu.
